text: Surgery of the pancreas remains one of the most talented fields in general surgery. There is only scarce data on robotic approach of pancreatic lesions. Tumors of pancreatic tail can be resected laparoscopically with good results. Robotics may offer a more stable platform for dissection and mobilization of such tumors, especially when the spleen should be preserved. Whipple pancreatodermatoma is a long and challenging procedure which may be facilitated from robotics. We present here our experience with robotic pancreatic cases including six patients that underwent distal pancreatectomy as in the video already showing and two of them with spleen preservation. In one case There was a large pancreatic cyst removal and two cases that underwent the WIPI pancretodermectomy using a totally robotic technique. Based on an extensive experience with paroscoping and robotic operations and after a series of successful digital pancreatectomies and hybrid pancretodermectomies, we established a totally robotic technique for WIPI pancretodermectomy.

The surgeon performs advanced dissection near the splenic hilum and above the pancreas, identifying major splenic trunks. Using vessel loops and da Vinci instruments, the surgeon carefully controls and dissects around each vessel to prevent bleeding, especially to protect the spleen. The pancreatic tail is dissected free from surrounding tissues, creating a plane for division. The pancreatic tail, including a benign ectopic splenic mass, is divided using a linear stapler.

The surgeon removes the specimen using an ento bag. The duodenum is lifted to extend the Kocher maneuver, facilitating access to the right abdomen and the root of the mesentery. The fore portion of the duodenum and the duodenal-jejunal junction are visible, with the jejunum being freed as the Whipple specimen is prepared for removal.

The surgeon divides the common bile duct and removes the stent, completing the cholecystectomy. After removing the specimen through a small extension of the right upper robotic incision, pneumoperitoneum is regained by suturing the incision and redocking the robotic system. The continuity of the gastrointestinal tract is repaired with a pancreaticogastric anastomosis and a duodenal-jejunal anastomosis, using a linear stapler and robotic needle holders for suturing. The creation of the biliodigestive anastomosis is performed with precision using a robotic hook, showcasing the final result of the operation.
Thank you for your attention. Although our presentation includes use of the DaVinci SI system, we have no disclosures to make. Thank you very much.
